questions.prompt: Intellectual movement emphasizing classical learning and human potential.
questions.explanation: Renaissance humanism (~1350-1600) revived Greek and Roman texts to argue that human beings were fit subjects of study and celebration. Scholars like Petrarch coined 'studia humanitatis' (grammar, rhetoric, history, poetry, moral philosophy), shifting education from scholasticism to classical models. AP Euro links humanism to IDS and SCD: it underpins the visual revolution of Donatello and Leonardo, secular political thought (Machiavelli), and the critique of Church corruption that fed the Reformation.

questions.prompt: Financial support from the Medici family that helped Renaissance art and learning flourish.
questions.explanation: The Medici banking dynasty of Florence used their commercial wealth to fund Botticelli, Michelangelo, and Leonardo da Vinci, turning Florence into the Renaissance's cultural capital. Lorenzo de' Medici ('the Magnificent') maintained a Neoplatonist academy. AP Euro uses the Medici to show how ECD wealth enabled SCD cultural patronage and how Italian city-state prosperity drove the Renaissance before Habsburg-French invasions disrupted it.

questions.prompt: Renaissance belief that educated citizens should serve the public good.
questions.explanation: Civic humanism, articulated by Leonardo Bruni (c.1370-1444), argued that the highest human purpose was active participation in political life rather than monastic withdrawal. Bruni celebrated Florence's republican government as heir to ancient Rome. AP Euro connects civic humanism to IDS (identity of the educated citizen) and SCD (changing views of the individual's relationship to the state), foreshadowing Enlightenment ideas of citizenship.

questions.prompt: Technology that spread books, ideas, and religious debate.
questions.explanation: Johannes Gutenberg's movable-type press (c.1450, Mainz) reduced the cost of books by roughly 80% within decades, making vernacular Bibles, humanist texts, and political pamphlets accessible to literate laypeople. By 1500 over 8 million books had been printed in Europe. AP Euro treats the press as a transformative SCD and IDS force: it accelerated the Reformation (Luther's 95 Theses spread in weeks), standardized vernacular languages, and created Europe's first mass-media public sphere.

questions.prompt: Renaissance movement emphasizing Christian humanism and reform.
questions.explanation: The Northern Renaissance (~1480-1560) carried Italian humanism into Germany, France, England, and the Low Countries, fusing it with Christian reform priorities. Erasmus (Praise of Folly, 1511) and Thomas More (Utopia, 1516) used classical tools to critique Church corruption without breaking with Rome. AP Euro distinguishes it from the Italian Renaissance by its greater religiosity (IDS), emphasis on Biblical scholarship, and direct intellectual lineage to the Protestant Reformation.

questions.prompt: Christian humanist who criticized corruption and emphasized education.
questions.explanation: Erasmus of Rotterdam (1466-1536) produced the first printed Greek New Testament (1516), whose textual corrections embarrassed the Church, and satirized clerical abuses in Praise of Folly. Unlike Luther, he never broke from Catholicism, famously clashing with Luther over free will. AP Euro uses Erasmus to illustrate 'reformation without rupture' and the intellectual bridge between the Northern Renaissance and the Protestant Reformation (IDS, SCD).

questions.prompt: Urban centers like Florence and Venice that supported commerce and patronage.
questions.explanation: Northern Italy's fragmented geography produced independent city-states — Florence, Venice, Milan, Naples, and the Papal States — each governed as a republic or signoria (despotism). Their commercial rivalry and absence of a centralizing monarchy created conditions that funded Renaissance art and scholarship. AP Euro uses Italian city-states to explain why the Renaissance began in Italy: ECD wealth from banking and trade produced the IDS cultural flowering.

questions.prompt: Stronger centralized monarchies that expanded royal authority in early modern Europe.
questions.explanation: Historians use 'new monarchies' to describe the consolidating royal states of late-15th-century England (Henry VII), France (Louis XI), and Spain (Ferdinand and Isabella), which curtailed noble power through paid bureaucracies, professional armies, and royal courts. AP Euro ties new monarchies to NEI (state-building through religious uniformity), ECD (taxation funding military expansion), and the groundwork for later absolutism.

questions.prompt: Renaissance emphasis on human potential, achievement, and personal distinction.
questions.explanation: Jacob Burckhardt's 1860 thesis argued the Renaissance produced a new individual who could 'self-fashion' identity through education, art, and achievement rather than birth. Cellini's autobiography and portrait painting reflected this ethos. Modern historians qualify Burckhardt, but AP Euro still uses individualism as an IDS concept to contrast Renaissance culture with medieval collectivist piety and to trace its Enlightenment legacy in concepts of natural rights.

questions.prompt: Agreement dividing Atlantic claims between Spain and Portugal.
questions.explanation: Brokered by Pope Alexander VI and signed in 1494, the Treaty of Tordesillas divided the non-Christian world between Spain and Portugal along a meridian 370 leagues west of Cape Verde: Spain received everything to the west, Portugal everything to the east. It reflected papal authority over international affairs — authority England, France, and the Netherlands simply ignored. AP Euro uses it to test INT (diplomacy) and the limits of papal power in IDS.

questions.prompt: Reducing the role of religion in government.
questions.explanation: Renaissance secularism describes the growing tendency to explain human affairs through natural rather than supernatural causes. Machiavelli's The Prince (1513) analyzed power without reference to Christian ethics; portraiture celebrated individual achievement. AP Euro frames secularism as an IDS shift: the gradual displacement of a God-centered worldview by a human-centered one, with full implications played out in the Scientific Revolution and Enlightenment.

questions.prompt: Centralizing Catholic rulers who sponsored exploration and built a powerful early modern empire.
questions.explanation: Ferdinand of Aragon and Isabella of Castile unified Spain by their 1469 marriage, then consolidated power by expelling Jews (1492), converting or expelling Muslims, and sponsoring Columbus. Their grandson Charles I became Holy Roman Emperor Charles V, making Spain the 16th century's dominant power. AP Euro uses the Spanish monarchy to illustrate NEI (state-building via religious uniformity), ECD (silver empire), and INT (Habsburg hegemony over Europe).

questions.prompt: Writing in everyday local languages rather than Latin.
questions.explanation: Vernacular literature — writing in everyday spoken languages rather than Latin — gained prestige during the Renaissance and Reformation. Dante's Divine Comedy (Italian), Chaucer's Canterbury Tales (English), and Luther's German Bible (1534) standardized national languages. AP Euro tracks vernacular literature as an IDS marker of national identity formation and SCD factor: the printing press multiplied vernacular texts, spreading literacy beyond clerical elites.

questions.prompt: Middle class or owners of capital in Marxist theory.
questions.explanation: The bourgeoisie — merchants, bankers, lawyers, and skilled craftsmen living in towns — grew wealthy through Italy's commercial revolution and later through Atlantic trade. They funded Renaissance art, drove literacy, and increasingly challenged aristocratic privilege. AP Euro uses the term in ECD contexts to trace the rise of capitalism and in NEI contexts to trace political challenges to traditional hierarchies, culminating in the French Revolution.

questions.prompt: Renaissance artistic technique creating realistic depth on a flat surface.
questions.explanation: Linear perspective, mathematically codified by Filippo Brunelleschi around 1415 and theorized by Leon Battista Alberti in De pictura (1435), gave Renaissance painters a geometric technique for creating the illusion of three-dimensional depth on a flat surface using a single vanishing point. Masaccio, Leonardo, and Raphael mastered it. AP Euro uses perspective as an SCD marker: humanist confidence in human reason's ability to measure the world — a visual counterpart to scientific empiricism.

questions.prompt: Spanish labor system that exploited Indigenous people while claiming to protect and Christianize them.
questions.explanation: The encomienda system granted Spanish conquistadors the labor and tribute of a fixed number of Indigenous people in exchange for their supposed Christianization. In practice it drove brutal exploitation: Caribbean Indigenous populations fell by over 90% within decades. Las Casas's 1542 Brief Account of the Destruction of the Indies prompted the New Laws limiting encomienda. AP Euro uses encomienda to test NEI (colonialism) and ECD (extractive Atlantic economy).

questions.prompt: Forced migration of Africans to labor in the Americas.
questions.explanation: The Atlantic slave trade (c.1500-1800s) forcibly transported approximately 12.5 million Africans to the Americas, with roughly 10.7 million surviving the Middle Passage. Portugal dominated early trade; by the 18th century Britain was the largest carrier. Enslaved Africans produced sugar, tobacco, and cotton funding European commercial capitalism. AP Euro frames the trade as an ECD driver (plantation profits) and NEI theme (racism, colonial violence, and resistance).

questions.prompt: Maritime expansion around Africa into the Indian Ocean.
questions.explanation: Portugal pioneered systematic Atlantic and Indian Ocean exploration under Prince Henry 'the Navigator' (d.1460). Bartolomeu Dias rounded the Cape of Good Hope in 1488; Vasco da Gama reached India in 1498, breaking the Ottoman/Venetian spice-route monopoly. AP Euro links Portugal to ECD (first global trade network), INT (shifting power from Mediterranean to Atlantic), and NEI (empire and indigenous encounters).

questions.prompt: An economy historically organized around large cash-crop farms.
questions.explanation: The plantation economy organized large-scale export agriculture in the Americas around enslaved African labor growing sugar (Caribbean, Brazil), tobacco (Virginia), and later cotton. Portuguese Brazil led sugar production by 1600; by the 18th century Saint-Domingue produced 40% of Europe's sugar. AP Euro connects the plantation economy to ECD (mercantile profits) and NEI (racial slavery and resistance) and to the European demand for the Atlantic slave trade.

questions.prompt: Exchange of plants, animals, people, diseases, and ideas between hemispheres.
questions.explanation: Historian Alfred Crosby coined 'Columbian Exchange' for the post-1492 transfer of plants, animals, pathogens, and peoples between hemispheres. Europe received tomatoes, potatoes, maize, and tobacco; the Americas received smallpox, measles, horses, and cattle. Smallpox alone killed an estimated 50-90% of Indigenous populations. European population growth after 1600 owed partly to New World calorie crops. AP Euro tests this under ECD (global trade) and NEI (demographic catastrophe).

questions.prompt: Political thinker who wrote The Prince.
questions.explanation: Niccolò Machiavelli (1469-1527) wrote The Prince (1513) arguing that a ruler must use force and cunning — like a lion and a fox — and that it is better to be feared than loved if one cannot be both. His secular, amoral analysis broke with the Christian 'mirror-for-princes' tradition. AP Euro treats Machiavelli as an IDS pivot: the beginning of modern political science divorced from theology, and an early articulation of raison d'etat.

questions.prompt: Business funded by investors who shared profits and risks.
questions.explanation: Joint-stock companies pooled investment from multiple shareholders, distributing profits and losses proportionally — allowing merchants to fund risky overseas ventures without personal ruin. The Dutch East India Company (VOC, 1602) and English East India Company (1600) controlled trade routes and exercised quasi-governmental authority. AP Euro uses joint-stock companies as an ECD innovation driving Atlantic capitalism and an INT mechanism extending European power globally.

questions.prompt: Expansion of banking, trade, joint-stock companies, and capitalism.
questions.explanation: The Commercial Revolution (~1300-1700) describes the shift from subsistence to market-oriented production, funded by Italian banking innovations (bills of exchange, double-entry bookkeeping, credit), and later by Atlantic trade expansion. It produced the bourgeoisie, joint-stock companies, and eventually mercantile empires. AP Euro treats the Commercial Revolution as the ECD foundation for both the Columbian expansion and the later Industrial Revolution.

questions.prompt: Inflation linked to population growth and New World silver.
questions.explanation: The Price Revolution (~1500-1650) saw European prices rise 400-600% over 150 years, driven primarily by New World silver flooding through Seville into the European money supply and compounded by population growth. Fixed-rent lords lost purchasing power while merchants with flexible incomes profited. AP Euro links it to ECD (destabilizing feudal economies, accelerating capitalism) and SCD (social upheaval), distinguishing it from modern inflation by its century-long timeframe.

questions.prompt: American bullion that enriched Spain, expanded global trade, and contributed to inflation in Europe.
questions.explanation: The silver mines of Potosi (Peru, discovered 1545) and Zacatecas (Mexico) produced roughly 80% of the world's silver between 1500 and 1800, extracted by the mita forced-labor system at tremendous human cost. Spain funneled this bullion through Seville into European markets, funding its wars but fueling the Price Revolution. AP Euro ties New World silver to ECD (mercantilism, inflation), NEI (colonial exploitation), and INT (Spain's military dominance).

questions.prompt: Reformer who challenged indulgences and launched Protestant reform.
questions.explanation: Martin Luther (1483-1546), an Augustinian friar and theology professor at Wittenberg, launched the Protestant Reformation when he posted his 95 Theses in 1517 disputing indulgence sales and papal authority. His three key treatises of 1520 (Address to the Christian Nobility, Babylonian Captivity, Freedom of a Christian) articulated sola fide, sola scriptura, and the priesthood of all believers. Protected by Elector Frederick of Saxony, Luther translated the Bible into German (1534), shaping the modern language.

questions.prompt: Luther's critique of indulgences and church practices.
questions.explanation: Luther's 95 Theses (October 31, 1517) were an academic disputation challenging the theology and sale of indulgences — Church pardons reducing time in purgatory — especially those sold by Johann Tetzel to fund St. Peter's Basilica in Rome. The printing press turned the Latin theses into a German-language popular sensation within weeks. AP Euro uses them as the IDS spark of the Reformation and a case study in how print culture transformed religious debate.

questions.prompt: Protestant tradition associated with predestination and disciplined communities.
questions.explanation: Calvinism, founded by John Calvin (1509-1564) in Geneva, held that God's sovereignty was absolute and unconditional — hence predestination. Calvin's Institutes of the Christian Religion (1536) became Protestantism's most systematic theology. Geneva became a 'Protestant Rome,' training missionaries sent to France (Huguenots), Scotland (Presbyterians), and the Netherlands (Dutch Reformed). AP Euro uses Calvinism to trace IDS (religious identity) and SCD (work ethic, capitalism debates) themes.

questions.prompt: Calvinist belief that God had already chosen who would be saved.
questions.explanation: Predestination held that God had, before the creation of the world, chosen (elected) some souls for salvation and condemned others — regardless of human merit or works. Calvin's double predestination was its most rigorous form. The doctrine created profound anxiety but also (per Max Weber) an intense worldly discipline: believers sought signs of election in diligent work and moral conduct. AP Euro uses predestination to test IDS (Reformed identity) and SCD (Weber's Protestant work ethic thesis).

questions.prompt: Early modern effort by churches and states to regulate household behavior and morality.
questions.explanation: Protestant reformers reconfigured the family as the basic unit of Christian society, elevating marriage (by abolishing celibacy for clergy) and the household as the site of religious education. Luther stressed the husband/father as 'priest' of his household; Calvinist Geneva punished adultery and idleness through consistory courts. AP Euro uses this under SCD: the Reformation simultaneously raised the spiritual status of marriage and intensified patriarchal control over women and children.

questions.prompt: Catholic renewal and response to Protestantism.
questions.explanation: The Catholic Reformation (also Counter-Reformation, c.1540-1648) was the Church's internal renewal and response to Protestantism, expressed through the Council of Trent (1545-1563), the Jesuit order, the Index of Forbidden Books, and the Roman Inquisition. Trent reaffirmed Catholic doctrines Luther had challenged (works, tradition, seven sacraments) while mandating seminary training for priests. AP Euro distinguishes genuine internal reform from purely defensive reaction to Protestantism.

questions.prompt: Church pardons sold to reduce punishment for sin.
questions.explanation: Indulgences were papal grants reducing the temporal punishment (time in purgatory) for sins already forgiven in confession. The systematic sale of indulgences — most notoriously by Johann Tetzel for the rebuilding of St. Peter's Basilica — sparked Luther's 95 Theses (1517). AP Euro uses indulgences to explain the immediate spark of the Reformation (IDS) and the broader critique of the Church's commercialization of salvation that made Luther's message resonate far beyond academic circles.

questions.prompt: Catholic council clarifying doctrine and reforming abuses.
questions.explanation: The Council of Trent (1545-1563) was the Catholic Church's comprehensive response to the Protestant challenge. It reaffirmed justification by faith and works (rejecting sola fide), upheld all seven sacraments, affirmed Latin Vulgate as the authoritative Bible, and mandated seminaries to improve clerical education. Trent defined Catholic doctrine for four centuries. AP Euro tests it as the institutional centerpiece of the Catholic Reformation and a key NEI event in the confessionalization process.

questions.prompt: Break between England and the Catholic Church under Henry VIII.
questions.explanation: Henry VIII broke with Rome in 1534 through the Act of Supremacy, making himself head of the Church of England, primarily to secure an annulment from Catherine of Aragon and produce a male heir. The English Reformation was thus driven more by dynastic politics than theology — making it unique among Protestant reformations. AP Euro uses it to illustrate how personal and political motives intersected with religious change (IDS, NEI), and to explain England's distinctive via media in religion.

questions.prompt: 1555 settlement allowing rulers to choose Lutheranism or Catholicism.
questions.explanation: The Peace of Augsburg (1555) ended the first German religious wars by establishing cuius regio, eius religio ('whose realm, his religion') — each German prince could choose Lutheranism or Catholicism for his territory, and subjects who disagreed could emigrate. It recognized Lutheranism legally but excluded Calvinism, planting seeds for the Thirty Years War. AP Euro uses Augsburg as a NEI milestone: the first European legal recognition of religious pluralism within a political framework.

questions.prompt: Spread of reading skills helped by printing, vernacular texts, and religious reform.
questions.explanation: Protestant reformers insisted that all Christians must read the Bible personally, creating strong incentives for vernacular literacy. Luther's German Bible (1534), Calvin's French Institutes, and the printing press together doubled Northern European literacy rates over the 16th century. AP Euro connects literacy growth to SCD (education, gender), IDS (Bible-reading Protestant identity), and the long-term roots of the Enlightenment's reading public.

questions.prompt: Bible translated into everyday language, allowing more laypeople to read scripture.
questions.explanation: Translating the Bible into vernacular languages was a central Reformation strategy for bypassing clerical intermediaries. Luther's German Bible (New Testament 1522, complete 1534) not only spread Protestant theology but helped standardize High German. William Tyndale produced the first English New Testament (1526). AP Euro uses vernacular Bible translation as an IDS marker (believers in direct relationship with God) and SCD evidence (literacy, nationalized religion).

questions.prompt: Dynastic struggle between Habsburg and French rulers that shaped sixteenth-century European politics.
questions.explanation: The Habsburg-Valois rivalry (c.1494-1559) pitted the French Valois kings against the Habsburg Holy Roman Emperors and Spanish kings in a series of Italian Wars for control of Italy and European hegemony. Francis I of France even allied with the Ottoman sultan against Charles V. The Peace of Cateau-Cambresis (1559) gave Spain dominance in Italy. AP Euro uses this rivalry to illustrate INT (dynastic politics overriding religious solidarity) and ECD (the cost of constant warfare).

questions.prompt: French decree that granted limited toleration to Huguenots after religious wars.
questions.explanation: Henry IV of France issued the Edict of Nantes in 1598, granting French Huguenots (Calvinists) the right to practice their faith privately, hold public office, and garrison specific towns — ending the French Wars of Religion. Louis XIV revoked it in 1685 (Edict of Fontainebleau), forcing 200,000 Huguenots to flee abroad, draining France of skilled artisans. AP Euro uses Nantes to test NEI (religious coexistence vs. persecution) and INT (skilled Huguenot emigration benefiting Dutch and English rivals).

questions.prompt: Religious movement that challenged Catholic authority.
questions.explanation: The Protestant Reformation (1517-c.1648) fractured Western Christian unity, spawning Lutheran, Calvinist, Anglican, and radical (Anabaptist) traditions that rejected papal authority and Roman doctrines. Powered by the printing press, vernacular Bibles, and the political support of German princes and Swiss city councils, it permanently ended the medieval Church's monopoly on Western Christianity. AP Euro themes: IDS (religious identity), NEI (religious wars), INT (Habsburg-Protestant conflict), SCD (family, literacy).

questions.prompt: English law making the monarch head of the Church of England.
questions.explanation: England's Act of Supremacy (1534) declared Henry VIII 'the only Supreme Head on Earth of the Church of England,' severing the English Church from Rome's jurisdiction. It was primarily a political not a doctrinal break — English theology remained largely Catholic until Edward VI's reign. Those who refused to swear loyalty (Thomas More, Bishop Fisher) were executed. AP Euro uses it to illustrate the intersection of dynastic politics and religious reform (IDS, NEI) and to explain English exceptionalism in the Reformation.

questions.prompt: Catholic order focused on education, missions, and defending Catholicism.
questions.explanation: Ignatius of Loyola (1491-1556) founded the Society of Jesus (Jesuits) in 1540, organizing it on quasi-military discipline with absolute obedience to the Pope. Jesuits became Catholicism's elite educational and missionary force: they founded hundreds of universities, reconverted much of southern Germany and Poland, and evangelized Asia, Africa, and the Americas. AP Euro uses Jesuits to illustrate the Catholic Reformation's reach (IDS, INT) and the connection between education and confessional state-building.

questions.prompt: Regional rulers in the Holy Roman Empire who used the Reformation to assert power.
questions.explanation: German princes within the Holy Roman Empire supported Luther partly from genuine religious conviction and partly because the Reformation offered political leverage: confiscating Church lands enriched princely coffers, and religious dissent justified defying Emperor Charles V. The Schmalkaldic League (1531) formalized Protestant princes' military alliance. AP Euro uses the German princes to explain why the Reformation survived in Germany — without political protection it would have been suppressed like Hus's movement a century earlier.

questions.prompt: Conflicts fueled by religious division and political power.
questions.explanation: The French Wars of Religion (1562-1598) pitted Catholic leagues against Huguenot nobles in eight intermittent civil wars, killing perhaps 3 million. The St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre (1572) — in which ~10,000 Huguenots were slaughtered — shocked Europe. Religious wars across the Holy Roman Empire, the Netherlands, and France intertwined dynastic ambition with confessional conflict. AP Euro frames them as the destructive consequence of Reformation-era IDS fragmentation (NEI, INT).

questions.prompt: Catholic order focused on education, missions, and defending Catholic doctrine.
questions.explanation: Ignatius of Loyola (1491-1556) founded the Society of Jesus (Jesuits) in 1540, organized on quasi-military discipline with absolute obedience to the Pope. Jesuits became Catholicism's elite educational and missionary force, founding hundreds of universities and reconverting much of Poland, southern Germany, and parts of France. AP Euro uses the Jesuits to illustrate the Catholic Reformation's reach (IDS) and the connection between education, confessionalization, and political power (NEI, INT).

questions.prompt: Protestant doctrine that salvation comes through faith alone rather than good works.
questions.explanation: Sola fide ('by faith alone') was Luther's core theological breakthrough, drawn from Romans 1:17: sinners are justified — made right with God — by faith alone, not by works, sacraments, or Church mediation. This directly challenged the Catholic doctrine that grace came through the seven sacraments administered by ordained priests. AP Euro treats sola fide as the IDS fulcrum of the Reformation: it made every believer directly responsible to God, undermining clerical authority and the entire sacramental economy.

questions.prompt: Protestant idea that Christians could approach God directly without a special clerical class.
questions.explanation: Luther's doctrine of the priesthood of all believers held that every baptized Christian had direct access to God without requiring priestly mediation — erasing the spiritual distinction between clergy and laity. This democratized religious authority in theory, though Luther quickly moved to constrain lay interpretation when radical reformers (Anabaptists, peasants) drew revolutionary social conclusions. AP Euro uses this to test IDS (Protestant identity) and SCD (Reformation's ambiguous legacy for social hierarchy).

questions.prompt: Process by which states and churches organized society around official religious identities.
questions.explanation: Confessionalization (Konfessionalisierung) describes the 16th-17th century process by which Catholic, Lutheran, and Calvinist churches and states worked together to define doctrinal orthodoxy, enforce moral discipline, and shape subjects' identities. Ernst Walter Zeeden and Heinz Schilling developed this historiographical concept. AP Euro uses confessionalization to explain how the Reformation gave birth to more intrusive, bureaucratic states (NEI, IDS) and how religious identity became a marker of political loyalty.

questions.prompt: Destruction or rejection of religious images, often linked to Reformation conflicts.
questions.explanation: Iconoclasm — the destruction of religious images, statues, and altarpieces — erupted repeatedly during the Reformation, as Calvinist and Zwinglian reformers denounced religious images as idolatry. The Bildersturm swept Swiss and German cities in the 1520s; the Netherlands experienced a massive iconoclastic wave in 1566. AP Euro uses iconoclasm as an SCD and IDS indicator of how deeply Reformation theology reshaped visual culture and popular piety, often through violent popular action.

questions.prompt: Persecutions fueled by religious anxiety, social tension, and local accusations in early modern Europe.
questions.explanation: European witch trials (~1450-1700) executed an estimated 40,000-60,000 people — roughly 75-80% women — peaking during the religious and economic crises of the Thirty Years War era. The Malleus Maleficarum (1487) systematized witch-hunting theology. Historians link the persecution to confessionalization (both Catholics and Protestants prosecuted witches), social anxieties over gender, and community tensions. AP Euro uses witch hunts to test SCD (gender, community), IDS (religious anxiety), and the limits of state order.

questions.prompt: Palace used to display royal power and control nobles.
questions.explanation: Louis XIV transformed a hunting lodge at Versailles into Europe's grandest palace (construction 1661-1710), housing the entire French court — some 10,000 people — and making attendance there mandatory for great nobles. Versailles served as a political tool: nobles absorbed in courtly ritual and competition had no time to plot rebellion. AP Euro uses Versailles to illustrate absolutist propaganda (IDS, SCD) and the taming of the nobility (NEI), and notes that its enormous cost strained French finances.

questions.prompt: The system where monarchs claim centralized authority.
questions.explanation: Absolutism describes the concentration of legislative, judicial, and executive power in the monarch's person, justified by divine right theology and practical state-building. France under Louis XIV ('L'etat c'est moi') is the textbook case; historians debate whether it was truly 'absolute' or limited by noble, clerical, and legal custom. AP Euro uses absolutism as an NEI and IDS theme: it appears in contrast with Dutch/English constitutionalism and in debates about sovereignty that run from Bodin through Hobbes to Locke.

questions.prompt: The system where law and representative institutions limit royal power.
questions.explanation: A constitutional monarchy limits the ruler's power through a fundamental law (written or unwritten) and representative institutions. England after 1689 became the era's exemplary constitutional monarchy: the Crown reigned but Parliament governed. Theorized by Locke and admired by Montesquieu, the English model influenced Enlightenment political thought and later American and French constitutionalism. AP Euro contrasts it with absolute monarchy (NEI) and traces its IDS implications for concepts of citizenship and rights.

questions.prompt: French absolute monarch associated with Versailles.
questions.explanation: Louis XIV (r.1643-1715) embodied French absolutism: he called himself the Sun King, built Versailles as a monument to royal glory, revoked the Edict of Nantes (1685) expelling Huguenots, and fought four major wars to expand France's borders. He domesticated the nobility by requiring their attendance at Versailles while provincial intendants administered France. AP Euro uses Louis XIV to test NEI (absolutism model), INT (French hegemony and the balance-of-power response), and ECD (Colbert's mercantilism).

questions.prompt: Commercial republic known for trade, finance, and religious toleration.
questions.explanation: The Dutch Republic (1581-1795) was Europe's foremost commercial and financial power in the 17th century — its Golden Age. A federal republic governed by merchant oligarchies (regents) rather than a king, it pioneered religious toleration, freedom of the press, and the Amsterdam Exchange Bank (1609). Rembrandt and Vermeer flourished in this bourgeois culture. AP Euro uses the Dutch Republic as the NEI countermodel to French absolutism: wealth, power, and cultural brilliance without absolute monarchy.

questions.prompt: Principle that the legislature holds supreme lawmaking authority.
questions.explanation: Parliamentary sovereignty — the principle that Parliament, not the monarch, holds supreme legal authority — was the constitutional outcome of England's 17th-century conflicts. The Glorious Revolution (1688) and Bill of Rights (1689) established that the Crown could not levy taxes, suspend laws, or maintain a standing army without Parliament's consent. AP Euro uses parliamentary sovereignty as the NEI and IDS counterpoint to French absolutism, and as the political foundation for later liberal theory.

questions.prompt: The conflict between king and Parliament over power.
questions.explanation: The English Civil War (1642-1651) pitted King Charles I — invoking divine right and ship money taxes — against a Parliament demanding constitutional limits on royal power. Parliamentary 'Roundhead' forces under Oliver Cromwell defeated and executed Charles I (1649), establishing the Commonwealth. The conflict ended with the Restoration (1660) and was definitively resolved by the Glorious Revolution (1688). AP Euro uses it to test NEI (English constitutional development) and IDS (the role of Puritanism in political conflict).

questions.prompt: This kind of monarchy has a Constitution and elected legislature limiting royal power.
questions.explanation: A limited monarchy constrains royal power through constitutional law and representative assemblies — contrasting with absolute monarchy where the ruler faces no legal checks. England's Magna Carta (1215) began this tradition; the Glorious Revolution (1688) consolidated it. AP Euro uses limited monarchy to test NEI comparisons between English and Continental political systems, and as context for Enlightenment theorists like Locke and Montesquieu who held it up as a model for rational governance.

questions.prompt: Document limiting royal power and protecting parliamentary rights.
questions.explanation: The English Bill of Rights (1689), passed after the Glorious Revolution removed James II, established that monarchs could not suspend laws, levy taxes, or maintain a standing army without Parliament's consent, guaranteed free elections, and prohibited cruel and unusual punishment. It made William III and Mary II constitutional monarchs. AP Euro uses it as the NEI milestone of English constitutionalism and notes its influence on the American Bill of Rights (1791) and the French Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789).

questions.prompt: Belief that monarchs receive authority from God.
questions.explanation: The doctrine that monarchs rule by God's will, not popular consent, justified their unchecked power and made rebellion a sin. James I of England wrote The True Law of Free Monarchies (1598) articulating this view. AP Euro contrasts divine right with Locke's social contract theory and uses it to explain the English Civil War: Parliament insisted on its constitutional role while Charles I insisted his power came from God alone, a conflict resolved at his execution in 1649.

questions.prompt: Theory that kings receive authority from God and are not accountable to subjects.
questions.explanation: Divine right theory held that monarchs received their authority directly from God, not from the consent of the governed, making rebellion against the king equivalent to rebellion against God. Bishop Bossuet formalized it in Politics Drawn from Holy Scripture (1709) for Louis XIV. AP Euro uses divine right to test IDS (political theology) in contrast with Locke's contract theory and in context of the English Civil War, where Charles I invoked it against a Parliament that beheaded him.

questions.prompt: Government system run by departments and officials.
questions.explanation: Absolutist state-building required professional bureaucracies to collect taxes, administer justice, and implement royal policy at the local level. Louis XIV's intendants (royal commissioners) replaced unreliable local nobles in the provinces; Frederick William I created the Prussian General War Commissariat. AP Euro uses bureaucracy as an NEI concept: the growth of state administrative capacity that defined early modern state-building, and later Enlightenment reformers' target for rationalization.

questions.prompt: 1688 event strengthening Parliament and limiting monarchy.
questions.explanation: The Glorious Revolution (1688-1689) removed the Catholic Stuart king James II and installed the Dutch Protestant William III of Orange and his wife Mary II, who accepted the Bill of Rights limiting royal prerogatives. It was 'glorious' because it was nearly bloodless in England (less so in Ireland and Scotland). AP Euro treats it as the NEI watershed establishing parliamentary sovereignty in England and as an INT event: a Dutch stadtholder becoming English king to check Louis XIV's France.

questions.prompt: Written set of laws that defines rules, punishments, rights, and authority.
questions.explanation: Legal codification — systematizing laws into a written code — was a hallmark of both absolutist rationalization and later Enlightenment reform. Louis XIV partially codified French commercial and criminal law under Colbert; Frederick the Great commissioned the Prussian General Code (1794); Napoleon's Code civil (1804) became the most influential. AP Euro uses legal codes to illustrate how states (NEI) and the Enlightenment (IDS) sought to replace feudal custom with uniform, rational rules governing property, contract, and persons.

questions.prompt: Process of concentrating power under a stronger central government.
questions.explanation: State centralization describes the 16th-18th century process by which European monarchs consolidated power at the expense of feudal lords, Church courts, and provincial autonomies — building standing armies, standardized law codes, and professional bureaucracies. France under Richelieu/Mazarin/Louis XIV and Prussia under the Great Elector are AP Euro's prime cases. The concept tests NEI: how modern states emerged from the fragmented medieval order, and ECD: how centralization enabled tax extraction.

questions.prompt: Russian ruler who westernized Russia and built St. Petersburg.
questions.explanation: Peter I of Russia (r.1682-1725) waged a systematic Westernization campaign: he built a new capital at St. Petersburg on Baltic marshland (1703), reorganized the army and navy on European models, forced nobles to shave their beards, and sent boyars abroad to study. His Table of Ranks (1722) tied noble status to state service. AP Euro uses Peter to test NEI (how Eastern Europe engaged or resisted Western models) and INT (Russia's emergence as a major power after defeating Sweden at Poltava, 1709).

questions.prompt: State-building pattern in which army strength and discipline shaped Prussian power.
questions.explanation: Prussia's 'Soldier King' Frederick William I (r.1713-1740) built Europe's most efficient military machine relative to the state's size, dedicating 80% of revenues to the army and personally drilling troops. His Junker (noble) officer corps made military service the highest Prussian virtue. AP Euro uses Prussian militarism to contrast with French-style absolutism (NEI) and to explain how a small, resource-poor state became a great power, anticipating Bismarck's 'blood and iron' policy.

questions.prompt: Idea that government legitimacy comes from an agreement among the people.
questions.explanation: Social contract theorists argued that legitimate government rested on an agreement between rulers and ruled. Hobbes (Leviathan, 1651) posited that subjects surrendered rights to an absolute sovereign to escape the brutal state of nature; Locke (Second Treatise, 1689) held that government must protect life, liberty, and property or forfeit its legitimacy; Rousseau (Social Contract, 1762) grounded sovereignty in the 'general will.' AP Euro uses social contract theory to test IDS: the intellectual foundation of liberal and revolutionary politics.

questions.prompt: Coerced or unequal labor system used to organize production and social hierarchy.
questions.explanation: While Western Europe gradually freed serfs after the Black Death, Eastern Europe (Russia, Poland, Prussia, Austria) experienced a 'second serfdom' from the 16th century onward: nobles legally bound peasants to the land to produce grain for export to Western markets. Russia's Sobornoye Ulozheniye (Law Code of 1649) formally enserfed the peasantry. AP Euro uses Eastern serfdom to test ECD (divergence of Western and Eastern agrarian economies) and NEI (noble-peasant power relations east of the Elbe).

questions.prompt: Prussian ruling dynasty that centralized authority and built a powerful military state.
questions.explanation: The Hohenzollern dynasty built Brandenburg-Prussia from a minor electorate to a great power over the 17th-18th centuries. The Great Elector Frederick William (r.1640-1688) centralized administration and built a standing army; Frederick I gained the royal title 'King in Prussia' (1701); Frederick William I created the Prussian military machine; Frederick II ('the Great') completed the rise. AP Euro uses the Hohenzollerns as the NEI case study for Eastern European state-building by war and bureaucracy.

questions.prompt: French elites who gained noble status through royal offices and bureaucracy.
questions.explanation: The noblesse de robe ('nobility of the robe') were French elites who purchased or inherited judicial and administrative offices — including seats in the parlements (law courts) — and received noble status as a result. They differed from the old sword nobility (noblesse d'epee) and frequently clashed with the Crown over taxation and royal edicts. AP Euro uses nobility of the robe to test NEI (the limits of absolutism) and SCD (social mobility through office-holding in early modern France).

questions.prompt: Profit-oriented farming using investment, enclosure, wage labor, and commercial production.
questions.explanation: Agricultural capitalism — profit-oriented farming using hired labor, enclosure, and capital investment — emerged in England and the Dutch Republic by the 17th century, replacing subsistence farming. English 'improving' landlords enclosed common lands, expelled smallholders, and raised sheep for wool or grew grain for urban markets. AP Euro uses agricultural capitalism to test ECD (the agrarian roots of the Industrial Revolution) and SCD (the displacement of peasants into towns).

questions.prompt: Economic system centered on trade, markets, investment, and profit before full industrialization.
questions.explanation: Commercial capitalism, dominant roughly 1400-1750, centered on merchants who bought goods cheap and sold dear, funding voyages, colonial ventures, and manufactures through commercial credit. The Italian city-states pioneered it; the Dutch Republic perfected it. AP Euro contrasts commercial capitalism (ECD) with later industrial capitalism and uses it to explain the accumulation of wealth that funded the Industrial Revolution, and the social emergence of a merchant bourgeoisie that challenged feudal hierarchies (NEI).

questions.prompt: Economic policy using state control and colonies to build national wealth.
questions.explanation: Mercantilism held that national wealth depended on accumulating gold and silver through a favorable trade balance (exporting more than importing) and that the state should actively manage the economy toward this goal. Jean-Baptiste Colbert, Louis XIV's finance minister, exemplified mercantilism: he promoted French manufacturing, imposed high tariffs, and used colonies as captive markets. AP Euro uses mercantilism to test ECD and NEI: state-directed capitalism before Adam Smith's free-market critique in Wealth of Nations (1776).

questions.prompt: System where private collectors paid rulers for the right to collect taxes.
questions.explanation: Tax farming was the system by which states sold the right to collect specific taxes to private entrepreneurs (tax farmers) who paid the Crown a lump sum upfront and profited by collecting more than they paid. France's fermiers generaux were notorious for efficiency and corruption alike. AP Euro uses tax farming to test NEI (state fiscal capacity in pre-bureaucratic systems) and ECD (private intermediaries in early modern finance), and to explain the fiscal grievances that fueled the French Revolution.

questions.prompt: Astronomer who proposed heliocentrism.
questions.explanation: Nicolaus Copernicus (1473-1543), a Polish canon, proposed in De revolutionibus orbium coelestium (published 1543, the year of his death) that the Earth and planets orbit the Sun — overturning the Ptolemaic geocentric model endorsed by the Church for over 1,000 years. He feared Church censure and held publication until his death. AP Euro uses Copernicus as the starting point of the Scientific Revolution (SCD, IDS) and to test how new cosmologies challenged established religious and intellectual authority.

questions.prompt: Shift toward observation, experimentation, and mathematical laws.
questions.explanation: The Scientific Revolution (~1543-1687) replaced Aristotelian natural philosophy and Ptolemaic cosmology with observation-based, mathematically expressed natural law. Key figures: Copernicus (heliocentrism), Vesalius (human anatomy), Kepler (planetary orbits), Galileo (telescope, mechanics), Harvey (blood circulation), and Newton (universal gravitation). AP Euro treats it as an SCD and IDS watershed: it established empirical method as the gold standard for knowledge and undermined the Church's authority to define natural truth.

questions.prompt: Scientist whose observations supported heliocentrism.
questions.explanation: Galileo Galilei (1564-1642) used the telescope to observe Jupiter's moons, sunspots, and the Moon's irregular surface — empirical evidence destroying Aristotelian celestial perfection. His Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems (1632) championed Copernicus and earned him a 1633 Inquisition trial, where he recanted under threat of torture. His conflict with the Church became the canonical AP Euro story of science challenging religious authority (IDS, SCD), though historians caution it was also a political dispute.

questions.prompt: Scientist known for laws of motion and universal gravitation.
questions.explanation: Isaac Newton (1643-1727) synthesized the Scientific Revolution in Principia Mathematica (1687), articulating three laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation — a single mathematical framework governing both earthly mechanics and planetary orbits. The Enlightenment idolized Newton as proof that human reason could decode nature's laws. AP Euro uses Newton to test SCD: the conviction that rational, mathematical inquiry could explain everything, including society — directly inspiring Enlightenment social science.

questions.prompt: Organizations that promoted research, publication, experimentation, and scholarly exchange.
questions.explanation: Scientific academies — the Royal Society of London (founded 1660) and the French Academie des Sciences (1666) — institutionalized the new science by providing venues for peer review, publication (Philosophical Transactions, 1665 — the first scientific journal), and state patronage of research. AP Euro uses scientific academies to test SCD: the professionalization of science, its patronage networks, and how state support of science linked Enlightenment rational improvement to royal prestige.

questions.prompt: Thinker associated with natural rights and consent of the governed.
questions.explanation: John Locke (1632-1704) argued in Two Treatises of Government (1689) that people possess natural rights to life, liberty, and property; that government's sole purpose is protecting these rights; and that when it fails, revolution is justified. Written to justify the Glorious Revolution, Locke's theory became the intellectual backbone of liberal democracy. AP Euro tests Locke under IDS (natural rights theory) and NEI (the philosophical foundation of English constitutionalism and later American and French revolutionary ideology).

questions.prompt: Thinker associated with separation of powers.
questions.explanation: Charles-Louis de Secondat, Baron de Montesquieu (1689-1755), analyzed global legal and political systems in The Spirit of the Laws (1748), concluding that a system of separated powers (executive, legislative, judicial) with mutual checks was the best guarantee of liberty — derived from his idealized reading of the English constitution. His model directly influenced the U.S. Constitution. AP Euro uses Montesquieu to test IDS (political theory) and to explain how the English constitutional model spread through Enlightenment thought.

questions.prompt: Thinker associated with the social contract and popular sovereignty.
questions.explanation: Jean-Jacques Rousseau (1712-1778) argued in The Social Contract (1762) that legitimate authority rests on the 'general will' of the community — not individual rights — and that modern civilization corrupted natural human goodness. His romanticism of nature and critique of inequality made him the ideological ancestor of both democratic nationalism and totalitarianism. AP Euro uses Rousseau to test IDS (the tension between individual rights and collective will) and as intellectual background to the French Revolution's more radical phases.

questions.prompt: Thinker associated with religious toleration and free expression.
questions.explanation: Voltaire (Francois-Marie Arouet, 1694-1778) was the Enlightenment's most influential popularizer and its sharpest critic of religious intolerance and judicial abuse. His Candide (1759) satirized Leibniz's optimism after the 1755 Lisbon earthquake; his Letters on the English held up English religious toleration and Newtonian science as models for France. His slogan 'Ecrasez l'infame!' ('Crush the infamous thing!') targeted clerical bigotry. AP Euro uses Voltaire to test IDS (secularism, toleration) and SCD (Enlightenment public opinion).

questions.prompt: Intellectual movement emphasizing reason, rights, and reform.
questions.explanation: The Enlightenment (~1680-1790) was a pan-European intellectual movement that applied Newtonian scientific method to human society, religion, and politics. Philosophes in France, Aufklarer in Germany, and moral philosophers in Scotland attacked superstition, religious intolerance, and arbitrary government while promoting reason, individual rights, and progress. AP Euro treats the Enlightenment as the IDS and SCD engine of modernity: it produced liberal political theory, the encyclopedia project, and the intellectual groundwork for the French Revolution.

questions.prompt: Thinker associated with separation of powers.
questions.explanation: Charles-Louis de Secondat, Baron de Montesquieu (1689-1755), analyzed global legal and political systems in The Spirit of the Laws (1748), concluding that a system of separated powers (executive, legislative, judicial) with mutual checks was the best guarantee of liberty — derived from his idealized reading of the English constitution. His model directly influenced the U.S. Constitution. AP Euro uses Montesquieu to test IDS (political theory) and to explain how the English constitutional model spread through Enlightenment thought.

questions.prompt: Belief that knowledge should come from observation and evidence.
questions.explanation: Empiricism holds that knowledge must be grounded in sensory experience and observation rather than a priori reasoning. Francis Bacon (Novum Organum, 1620) championed inductive method — gathering data through experiment to build general conclusions. John Locke applied empiricism to the mind, arguing that the human intellect begins as a blank slate (tabula rasa) shaped by experience. AP Euro uses empiricism to test SCD: it underpins the Scientific Revolution's method and challenges Aristotelian/scholastic deduction.

questions.prompt: Enlightenment approach that trusted reason as a path to knowledge.
questions.explanation: Rationalism holds that reason alone, independent of sensory experience, can yield fundamental truths. Rene Descartes (Discourse on Method, 1637) famously grounded his system in Cogito ergo sum ('I think, therefore I am') and sought mathematical certainty in all knowledge. Spinoza and Leibniz extended the tradition. AP Euro distinguishes rationalism from empiricism (though Newton and later thinkers blended both) and uses Descartes to test IDS: the conviction that systematic doubt and deductive reasoning can rebuild all human knowledge.

questions.prompt: Basic rights people are born with, such as life, liberty, and property.
questions.explanation: Natural rights theory held that human beings possess inherent rights — life, liberty, property (Locke); liberty, equality, property, security, resistance to oppression (French Declaration, 1789) — that exist prior to and above any government's law. Rooted in natural law philosophy (Grotius, Pufendorf) and Locke's Second Treatise, the concept powered both the American Declaration of Independence and the French Declaration of the Rights of Man. AP Euro tests natural rights as the IDS foundation of liberal political culture.

questions.prompt: Principle that government power comes from the people.
questions.explanation: Popular sovereignty holds that all legitimate governmental authority derives from the people, not from hereditary right or divine mandate. Rousseau's general will, the American Declaration's 'consent of the governed,' and the French Revolution's National Assembly all invoked popular sovereignty. AP Euro uses it to test IDS: the revolutionary idea that replaced divine right as the basis of political legitimacy, and as context for both constitutional experiments and the Terror's claim to speak for the 'people.'.

questions.prompt: Russian ruler associated with enlightened absolutism and territorial expansion.
questions.explanation: Catherine II of Russia (r.1762-1796) corresponded with Voltaire and Diderot, drafted a legal code inspired by Montesquieu (the Nakaz, 1767), and expanded Russia westward through Partitions of Poland and wars with the Ottomans. Yet the Pugachev Rebellion (1773-74) — the largest peasant uprising in Russian history — caused her to tighten serfdom rather than reform it. AP Euro uses Catherine to test NEI (enlightened absolutism) and the contradiction that philosophes celebrated autocrats who never enacted their advice.

questions.prompt: Prussian ruler associated with enlightened absolutism, military power, and reform.
questions.explanation: Frederick II of Prussia (r.1740-1786) called himself 'the first servant of the state,' wrote political philosophy, corresponded with Voltaire, promoted religious toleration, reformed law, and built the Prussian state bureaucracy — while launching wars (War of Austrian Succession, Seven Years War) that doubled Prussia's territory. He embodied 'enlightened absolutism': Enlightenment rhetoric married to ruthless Realpolitik. AP Euro uses Frederick to test NEI (state-building) and IDS (the limits of Enlightenment reform under autocracy).

questions.prompt: Austrian ruler whose Enlightenment-inspired reforms challenged traditional privileges.
questions.explanation: Joseph II of Austria (r.1765-1790) was the most radical enlightened despot: he abolished serfdom (1781), issued the Edict of Toleration (1781) granting Protestants and Jews limited rights, dissolved contemplative monasteries, and standardized law and language. His reforms generated such resistance from nobles, Church, and Belgians that many were reversed after his death. AP Euro uses Joseph II to test the limits of reform from above (NEI, IDS) — a case study in Enlightenment idealism colliding with entrenched privilege.

questions.prompt: Monarchical rule influenced by Enlightenment reform ideas.
questions.explanation: Enlightened absolutism described rulers who adopted Enlightenment vocabulary — reason, reform, welfare of the state — while retaining absolute power. Frederick the Great (Prussia), Catherine the Great (Russia), Joseph II (Austria), and Maria Theresa (Austria) exemplified this pattern. AP Euro uses enlightened absolutism to test NEI: these rulers modernized armies, legal codes, and economies without fundamentally challenging aristocratic privilege or creating representative institutions, illustrating the limits of top-down reform.

questions.prompt: Social gatherings where Enlightenment ideas spread.
questions.explanation: Parisian salons — regular gatherings hosted by educated aristocratic and bourgeois women (salonnières like Marie-Therese Rodet Geoffrin and Suzanne Necker) — were the social hub of Enlightenment intellectual life, where philosophes like Voltaire, Diderot, and Montesquieu debated and networked. AP Euro uses salons to test SCD: they expanded the 'public sphere' (Habermas's term), gave elite women a rare intellectual role, and demonstrate how ideas circulate through social networks rather than texts alone.

questions.prompt: Expansion of buying, advertising, and access to goods in early modern or modern society.
questions.explanation: The Consumer Revolution (~1650-1800) saw a dramatic expansion in the purchase of goods — textiles, ceramics, tea, sugar, furniture — across all social classes in Britain and Northwestern Europe, driven by rising incomes, advertising, and Atlantic trade. AP Euro uses the consumer revolution to test ECD: it linked colonial production (sugar, tobacco, cotton) to European demand, drove early industrialization in textile production, and created modern commercial culture, including fashion and advertising.

questions.prompt: Public gathering places where educated Europeans exchanged news, print culture, and Enlightenment ideas.
questions.explanation: English and Continental coffeehouses (~1650-1800) were cheap, egalitarian spaces where men (rarely women) could read newspapers, debate politics and commerce, and network across class lines for the price of a cup of coffee. They were called 'penny universities.' Lloyd's of London began as a coffeehouse; major newspapers and stock-jobbing firms emerged from them. AP Euro uses coffeehouses to test SCD: they are key physical nodes in Habermas's 'public sphere' where literate public opinion formed outside court and Church control.

questions.prompt: Space of debate created by print culture, coffeehouses, salons, and civic discussion.
questions.explanation: The 'public sphere' (Jurgen Habermas's concept) describes the social space — coffeehouses, salons, newspapers, pamphlets — that emerged in 18th-century Europe where private citizens came together to debate public affairs using reason rather than deference to authority. AP Euro uses it to test SCD and IDS: the public sphere explains how Enlightenment ideas circulated, created public opinion as a political force, and ultimately gave the Third Estate the conceptual tools to challenge the Old Regime.

questions.prompt: Communication form that spread ideas, identity, and criticism to wider audiences.
questions.explanation: Print culture — the world of newspapers, periodicals, pamphlets, and cheap books created by Gutenberg's press — expanded dramatically in the 17th-18th centuries. By 1700 London had multiple daily newspapers; French pamphlets debated royal policy. AP Euro uses print culture to test SCD: it created Habermas's 'public sphere,' spread Enlightenment ideas beyond elite circles, and directly powered both the Reformation (Luther's pamphlets) and the French Revolution (political journalism).

questions.prompt: Enlightenment reference work spreading secular and critical ideas.
questions.explanation: The Encyclopédie (1751-1772), edited by Denis Diderot and Jean le Rond d'Alembert with contributions from Voltaire, Rousseau, and Montesquieu, was Enlightenment thought's greatest monument: 28 volumes compiling secular, rational knowledge of arts, sciences, and trades. Twice banned by the French government, it circulated clandestinely. AP Euro tests the Encyclopédie under IDS and SCD: it embodied the philosophes' conviction that human reason could categorize all knowledge, and its secular tone directly challenged Church authority.

questions.prompt: Representative body called before the French Revolution.
choices: Declaration of the Rights of Man
questions.explanation: The Estates-General, France's dormant representative assembly of clergy (First Estate), nobility (Second Estate), and commoners (Third Estate), was summoned by Louis XVI in May 1789 — its first meeting since 1614 — to address France's bankruptcy from the American Revolutionary War and royal overspending. Disputes over voting procedure (by order vs. per capita) quickly radicalized the Third Estate into the National Assembly. AP Euro uses the Estates-General as the institutional trigger of the French Revolution (NEI, IDS).

questions.prompt: Principle that government power comes from the people.
choices: Declaration of the Rights of Man
questions.explanation: Popular sovereignty holds that legitimate government derives from the people's consent, not hereditary right or divine mandate. Articulated by Locke, Rousseau, and the French Declaration of Rights (1789), it became the Revolution's foundational IDS principle — but also justified the Terror when Robespierre claimed to speak for the 'general will.' AP Euro tests how popular sovereignty transformed politics from a monarchical to a democratic legitimacy framework, shaping every subsequent European revolution.

questions.prompt: Revolutionary body claiming to represent the French nation.
questions.explanation: When the Estates-General deadlocked over voting, the Third Estate renamed itself the National Assembly (June 17, 1789), declaring itself the true representative of the French people. On June 20, locked out of their chamber, delegates swore the Tennis Court Oath not to disperse until they had written a constitution. Louis XVI capitulated three days later. AP Euro uses the National Assembly as the NEI/IDS pivot: the moment popular sovereignty displaced royal authority as the foundation of legitimate government.

questions.prompt: French revolutionary statement of rights and citizenship.
choices: Declaration of the Rights of Man
questions.correctText: Declaration of the Rights of Man
questions.explanation: The Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen (August 26, 1789) proclaimed that sovereignty resides in the nation, that all men are born free and equal in rights, and that natural rights include liberty, property, security, and resistance to oppression. Heavily influenced by Locke and the American Declaration of Independence, it became the foundational IDS document of French and European liberalism. AP Euro tests it as the political creed of the Revolution's moderate phase.

questions.prompt: Mass conscription policy that mobilized French citizens for revolutionary war.
questions.explanation: The levee en masse (August 23, 1793) was the Committee of Public Safety's decree mobilizing the entire French nation for war: young men would fight, married men forge weapons, women make tents and nurse the wounded, children scrape lint for bandages. It raised an army of over 800,000 — dwarfing any professional force — and established the principle of citizen-soldier obligation. AP Euro uses it to test NEI (state capacity) and IDS (nationalism as military force), noting it transformed warfare permanently.

questions.prompt: French leader who consolidated reforms and built an empire.
questions.explanation: Napoleon Bonaparte (1769-1821) rose from Corsican-born artillery officer to Emperor of the French (1804-1814, 1815). He consolidated the Revolution's gains in the Napoleonic Code (1804), reformed French law, education, and administration, and conquered most of Europe before the Continental System, the Peninsular War, and the Russian campaign (1812) overstretched his empire. AP Euro tests Napoleon's dual legacy: he spread revolutionary principles (NEI) while destroying republics and imposing personal dictatorship (IDS).

questions.prompt: Loyalty to a nation that inspired unity and independence movements.
questions.explanation: Nationalism — the conviction that people sharing language, culture, or ethnicity constitute a 'nation' with the right to political self-determination — emerged from the French Revolution's identification of the people with the nation. The 19th century saw it become Europe's most powerful political force, driving Greek independence (1821-29), Italian unification (1861), German unification (1871), and Polish and Hungarian revolts. AP Euro tests nationalism under IDS and NEI: it dismantled empires, created new states, and ultimately contributed to both world wars.

questions.prompt: Revolution that challenged monarchy and privilege.
questions.explanation: The French Revolution (1789-1799) dismantled the Old Regime's social hierarchy and absolute monarchy, producing the Declaration of the Rights of Man, a constitutional monarchy, then the Republic, the Terror, and finally Napoleon. Rooted in Enlightenment ideas, fiscal crisis, and social grievances, it permanently established popular sovereignty and nationalism as Europe's dominant political forces. AP Euro treats it as the era's central NEI and IDS event, testing causes, phases (1789, 1792, 1793-94, Thermidor), and its European impact.

questions.prompt: Legal code that standardized law and property rights.
questions.explanation: The Napoleonic Code (Code Civil, 1804) standardized French law: it enshrined equality before the law, property rights, secular civil marriage and divorce, and freedom of religion — revolutionary gains — while reversing women's legal equality and reinforcing patriarchal authority in the family. Exported throughout Napoleon's empire (Netherlands, Italy, Spain, German states), it became the basis for legal systems across continental Europe and Latin America. AP Euro tests it under NEI (legal modernization) and SCD (gender).

questions.prompt: Successful enslaved people's revolt that created an independent Haiti.
questions.explanation: The Haitian Revolution (1791-1804) was the only successful slave revolt in history, transforming Saint-Domingue — France's wealthiest colony and the world's top sugar producer — into the independent Republic of Haiti. Toussaint Louverture led the uprising before his capture; Jean-Jacques Dessalines declared independence in 1804. AP Euro uses Haiti to test NEI (imperialism and its limits) and IDS (how revolutionary ideology contained contradictions that colonized peoples could deploy against European rulers).

questions.prompt: Conservative European order designed to suppress revolution and nationalism after Napoleon.
questions.explanation: Klemens von Metternich, Austrian Foreign Minister (and briefly Chancellor), dominated post-Napoleonic European diplomacy through the Congress of Vienna (1814-15) and the Concert of Europe's subsequent congresses. His system used great-power cooperation, collective intervention against revolutions, and a restored balance of power to prevent another French-style upheaval. AP Euro tests the Metternich system as the NEI conservative reaction to 1789 — effective until 1848 when revolutions swept it aside.

questions.prompt: Post-Napoleonic meeting restoring balance of power.
questions.explanation: The Congress of Vienna (September 1814 - June 1815) was the peace conference that reorganized Europe after Napoleon's defeat. Led by Metternich (Austria), Castlereagh (Britain), Tsar Alexander I (Russia), and Talleyrand (France), it restored legitimate monarchies, established a balance of power, created the German Confederation, and drew new borders. AP Euro tests it under INT (collective security, balance of power) and NEI (the conservative reaction to revolutionary nationalism that would fuel 1830 and 1848 uprisings).

questions.prompt: Post-Napoleonic diplomatic system designed to preserve balance of power and prevent revolution.
questions.explanation: The Concert of Europe was the great-power diplomatic system created at the Congress of Vienna (1814-15) among Austria, Prussia, Russia, and Britain (France joined 1818) to manage European stability through regular congresses and collective intervention against revolutionary threats. It met at Aix-la-Chapelle (1818), Troppau (1820), and Verona (1822). AP Euro uses the Concert as an INT concept: the first sustained experiment in collective security, lasting (with breaks) until 1914, and the direct ancestor of the League of Nations.

questions.prompt: Ideology favoring individual rights, constitutional government, and markets.
questions.explanation: 19th-century liberalism demanded constitutional government, individual rights, free markets, freedom of the press, and rule of law — drawing on Locke, Adam Smith, and the French Revolution's moderate phase. It was primarily the ideology of the educated bourgeoisie who wanted legal equality but feared democracy. AP Euro uses liberalism to test IDS: contrasting it with conservatism (which defended throne and altar) and socialism/radicalism (which demanded democracy and economic redistribution) — the ideological triplex of the 19th century.

questions.prompt: Radical phase of the French Revolution marked by executions.
questions.explanation: The Reign of Terror (September 1793 - July 1794) was the most radical phase of the French Revolution: Robespierre's Committee of Public Safety sent approximately 17,000 people to the guillotine and imprisoned 300,000 more, targeting perceived enemies of the Revolution. The Terror ended when Robespierre himself was arrested and executed in the Thermidorian Reaction (July 1794). AP Euro uses the Terror to test IDS (does 'popular sovereignty' justify authoritarian measures?) and NEI (the pathology of revolutionary radicalism).

questions.prompt: Urban working-class radicals who pushed the French Revolution toward price controls and republican politics.
questions.explanation: Sans-culottes ('without breeches') were Paris's urban working poor and artisans — the revolutionary crowd that stormed the Bastille (1789), marched to Versailles, and drove the radical phases of the Revolution. They demanded price controls on bread, popular democracy, and punishment of aristocrats and hoarders. Robespierre's Committee of Public Safety relied on sans-culotte energy during the Terror. AP Euro uses sans-culottes to test NEI (class dynamics of the Revolution) and IDS (the gap between bourgeois liberal leaders and radical popular demands).

questions.prompt: Social class shaped by economic role, work, wealth, and political interests.
questions.explanation: The industrial working class (proletariat) emerged in Britain's factory towns from the 1780s: men, women, and children working 12-16 hour days in dangerous textile mills, mines, and ironworks for subsistence wages. Friedrich Engels documented conditions in The Condition of the Working Class in England (1845). AP Euro uses the industrial working class to test ECD (industrial capitalism's social costs) and NEI (their political mobilization through Chartism, trade unions, and eventually socialist and labor parties).

questions.prompt: Rural panic in 1789 that drove peasants to attack feudal records and noble privileges.
questions.explanation: The Great Fear (July-August 1789) was a wave of rural panic and peasant violence that swept France when rumors spread that aristocrats were hiring brigands to destroy crops and starve the people. Peasants attacked noble chateaux and burned feudal records. It forced the National Assembly on August 4-5, 1789 to abolish feudal dues in the 'August Decrees.' AP Euro uses the Great Fear to test NEI (the social revolution beneath the political one) and SCD (the peasantry's role in dismantling the Old Regime).

questions.prompt: Ideology favoring tradition, monarchy, and social order.
questions.explanation: Edmund Burke's Reflections on the Revolution in France (1790) founded modern conservatism by arguing that society was an organic inheritance from the past — not a rational construct — and that reforming it by abstract principles (like 'rights of man') would produce chaos. Burke defended tradition, hierarchy, gradual change, and established institutions (Church, monarchy, Parliament). AP Euro uses conservatism to test IDS: the ideological triplex of the 19th century alongside liberalism and socialism, and Metternich's organizing principle.

questions.prompt: City-based protest, rioting, or disorder driven by food prices, work conditions, or political demands.
questions.explanation: Urban unrest — bread riots, political protests, and insurrections — was a recurring feature of 18th-19th century European cities whenever food prices spiked or political crises erupted. The sans-culottes' marches to Versailles (1789), the Luddite machine-breaking riots (1811-16), and Chartist demonstrations (1830s-40s) all reflected this pattern. AP Euro uses urban unrest to test NEI and SCD: it was the mechanism by which the urban poor exerted political pressure before universal suffrage.

questions.prompt: Artistic and intellectual movement emphasizing emotion, nature, imagination, and national feeling.
questions.explanation: Romanticism (c.1780-1850) was an artistic, literary, and intellectual movement that reacted against Enlightenment rationalism and industrial mechanization by valorizing emotion, imagination, nature, and the organic community of the nation. In literature: Wordsworth, Coleridge, Byron, Hugo, Goethe. In music: Beethoven, Schubert, Chopin. In painting: Delacroix, Caspar David Friedrich. AP Euro connects Romanticism to IDS (nationalist sentiment) and SCD (the cultural backlash against industrialization), and to the revolutionary nationalism of 1830 and 1848.

questions.prompt: Belief that people with shared identity should have political unity or self-rule.
questions.explanation: Revolutionary nationalism combined Romantic belief in the organic nation with liberal demands for constitutional government and self-determination. The Greek War of Independence (1821-29), Italian carbonari conspiracies, Polish uprisings, and the Revolutions of 1830 and 1848 all expressed this fusion. AP Euro uses revolutionary nationalism to test IDS: the idea that ethnic or cultural nations had a right to their own states — a principle that both threatened the multinational Habsburg and Ottoman empires and powered German and Italian unification.

questions.prompt: Wave of European uprisings driven by nationalism, liberalism, and social unrest.
questions.explanation: The Revolutions of 1848 swept across France, the German states, Austria-Hungary, Italy, and beyond in spring 1848, driven by a combination of liberal constitutionalism, romantic nationalism, and social grievances from industrial misery. France became a republic; the Frankfurt Parliament attempted German unification; Hungarian, Czech, and Italian nationalists rose against Habsburg rule. All failed by 1849, suppressed by conservative forces. AP Euro uses 1848 to test NEI: why the revolutions failed (class splits, nationalism vs. liberalism, Russian intervention).

questions.prompt: Political position seeking deep transformation of society, government, or rights.
questions.explanation: Radicalism demanded root-and-branch transformation of political and social structures — universal male (and eventually female) suffrage, abolition of hereditary privilege, and sometimes redistribution of wealth. Jacobins during the Terror, British Chartists, and continental democrats of 1848 exemplified it. AP Euro uses radicalism to test IDS: the left flank of 19th-century politics that pushed beyond liberal constitutionalism toward democratic and socialist demands, drawing on sans-culotte tradition and Rousseau.

questions.prompt: French constitutional monarchy created after the 1830 revolution and tied to bourgeois liberalism.
questions.explanation: The July Monarchy (1830-1848) was established when the 1830 July Revolution ousted the ultra-royalist Charles X and installed Louis-Philippe of Orleans as constitutional king — a compromise between the Old Regime and republicanism. It governed in the interests of the wealthy bourgeoisie but refused to extend suffrage, fueling discontent that produced the 1848 Revolution. AP Euro uses the July Monarchy to test NEI (how liberalism governed in practice) and IDS (the gap between liberal ideals and bourgeois class interest).

questions.prompt: Shift to machine-powered factory production.
questions.explanation: The Industrial Revolution (~1760-1850), beginning in Britain, transformed production from hand tools in homes and workshops to machine-powered factories. Key drivers: coal and iron, the steam engine (Watt, 1769), water-frame and spinning jenny textiles, and navigable canals/railways. Britain led because of coal deposits, capital from colonial trade, enclosure (rural labor supply), and parliamentary stability. AP Euro tests causes (ECD), social consequences (SCD, NEI), and the shift that made European global dominance possible.

questions.prompt: City wage laborers who worked in factories, mines, transport, and industrial services.
questions.explanation: Britain's Industrial Revolution created a new urban working class concentrated in factory towns like Manchester, Birmingham, and Leeds — living in squalid back-to-back housing, working 12-16 hours daily for subsistence wages. Friedrich Engels documented Manchester's conditions in 1845. AP Euro uses the urban working class to test ECD (industrial capitalism's human costs) and NEI: their political mobilization through Chartism, trade unions, and eventually socialist parties transformed European politics.

questions.prompt: Technology powering factories, mines, and railroads.
questions.explanation: James Watt's improved steam engine (1769, condensing chamber patent) made steam power efficient enough to drive factories, mines, and later railways and steamships — the central technology of the Industrial Revolution. By the 1830s steam engines powered cotton mills, iron furnaces, and locomotives. AP Euro uses the steam engine to test ECD: it was the 'general purpose technology' that allowed Britain to industrialize beyond textile workshops, and it explains why coal-rich regions (Britain, Belgium, Ruhr Valley) industrialized first.

questions.prompt: Production sector transformed by mechanization, factories, and industrial labor.
questions.explanation: The textile industry — especially cotton — was the engine of Britain's Industrial Revolution. The spinning jenny (Hargreaves, 1764), water frame (Arkwright, 1769), and power loom (Cartwright, 1785) mechanized production; by 1830 Manchester's mills supplied global markets.
